text: Upon inserting the scope, a large inflammatory mass is visible, involving the thickened cecum, appendices epiploicae, small bowel, and cecum base. Meticulous adhesiolysis is performed to identify the appendix within this mass.

Upon inserting the scope, the surgeon observes a large inflammatory mass in the right iliac fossa, consisting of the thickened cecum, appendices epiploicae, small bowel, and cecum base. Meticulous adhesiolysis is performed to identify the appendix within the mass.
The surgeon examines the anatomy, noting the firm texture of the cecum amidst localized peritonitis and inflammatory changes, which is visually evident in the surgical field.
The surgeon examines the anatomy, noting the firm texture of the cecum amidst localized peritonitis and inflammatory changes, which is visually evident in the surgical field.
The surgeon orients the surgical field and gradually reveals the tip of the appendix, which serves as a visual guide for further dissection.
The surgeon gradually exposes the tip of the appendix, which becomes visible amidst the inflamed surrounding tissues, aiding in anatomical orientation during the appendectomy.
The surgeon observes peritoneal flakes within the surgical field, indicating a previous perforation that appears to be currently sealed.
The surgeon observes peritoneal flakes in the surgical field, indicating a possible contained perforation of the appendix.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a blunt grasper or suction tip, carefully navigating the inflamed and firm tissue around the cecum.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a blunt grasper or suction tip, carefully maneuvering around the inflamed tissue near the appendix.
The surgeon observes early signs of inflammation around the perforation site, indicating that the perforation is not old.
The surgeon observes the appendix, noting early signs of inflammation and a recent perforation, with visible peritoneal flakes indicating a contained seal perforation.

The surgeon encounters thickened tissues due to inflammation, making them difficult to hold and handle. Minimal handling is advised to prevent excessive oozing, which could obscure the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters thickened tissues due to inflammation, making them difficult to hold and handle. Minimal handling is advised to prevent oozing and maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions involving the appendix epiploicae, which appear fibrinolytic and thickened. With meticulous dissection in the correct plane, the adhesions are peeled away, revealing underlying structures. Pus becomes visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions around the appendix epiploicae, which appear fibrinolytic and thickened. Meticulous dissection in the correct plane allows the peeling off of these adhesions, revealing underlying structures more clearly. Pus is visible entering the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies an unusual perforation located at the base of the appendix, visible amidst inflamed and thickened tissues.
The surgeon observes an unusual perforation located at the base of the appendix, distinct from the more common distal perforations.
The video shows the entire base of the appendix as gangrenous and sloughed out, with the cecum at the base also appearing gangrenous and sloughed out.
The video shows the base of the appendix and the adjacent cecum, both appearing gangrenous and sloughed out, indicating severe tissue damage.
The video shows the area of perforation at the base of the appendix, with visible sloughed out tissue in the cecum.
The video shows the area of perforation at the base of the appendix, with visibly sloughed out tissue extending to the cecum.
The surgeon applies pressure to the cecum, revealing pus emerging from the perforated area of sloughed tissue, indicating an active perforation that requires attention.
The surgeon applies pressure to the cecum, causing pus to emerge from the perforated area, indicating an active perforation that requires attention.
The surgeon mobilizes the cecum on the inferior wall region, assessing the friable and inflamed tissue to determine the best repair options.
The surgeon attempts to mobilize the cecum on the inferior wall region, revealing inflamed and friable tissue that requires careful consideration for suturing or alternative options.
The surgeon uses suction to remove sloughy tissue, carefully manipulating and identifying the extent of diseased tissue that needs to be excised.
The surgeon uses suction to remove sloughing tissue from the surgical field, carefully manipulating and identifying the extent of diseased tissue that needs to be excised.

The surgeon identifies remnant tissue in the plane between the cecum and the inferior mesentery, assessing its health for suturing. A large perforation is visible, likely contributing to the patient's lack of severe pain due to diabetes.
The surgeon identifies remnant tissue between the cecum and the inferior mesentery, assessing its health for potential suturing. A large perforation is visible in the area.
The surgeon observes that the entire tissue in the surgical field is sloughed out, indicating extensive tissue damage.
The surgeon observes that the entire tissue in the surgical field is sloughed out, indicating extensive tissue damage and necrosis.
The surgeon identifies a phlegmon or mass present in the appendix, characterized by inflamed and thickened tissue.
The surgeon observes a phlegmon or mass present in the appendix, indicating inflamed or infected tissue.
The surgeon encounters thick, fibrotic adhesions attached to the abdominal wall, which are being carefully manipulated and assessed.
The surgeon observes thick and fibrotic adhesions attached to the abdominal wall, indicating a challenging dissection environment.
The surgeon uses gauze to absorb blood and improve visibility in the surgical field, ensuring clearer visualization of the tissue by reducing light absorption from bloodstained areas.
The surgeon uses gauze to manage oozing, improving visibility by reducing bloodstains that could darken the surgical field.
The surgeon applies traction to mobilize the cecum, identifying avascular planes between the mesocolon and Gerota's fascia for safe tissue handling.
The surgeon applies traction to mobilize the cecum, identifying avascular planes between the mesocolon and Gerota's fascia for safe tissue handling.
The video shows the retroperitoneal tissues and the fascia covering them, providing a view of the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon exposes the retroperitoneal tissues and the fascia covering them, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.
The surgeon uses gauze to mop small areas of oozing blood, which visibly brightens the surgical field by removing bloodstains that darken the image.
The surgeon uses gauze to mop up small oozes, immediately brightening the surgical field and improving visibility.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection, carefully separating small tissue segments to ensure precise handling and exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection of small tissue segments, ensuring precision in separating these tissues during the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon gently removes sloughed out tissue to reveal the underlying area, focusing on precise identification of the affected region.
The surgeon gently removes sloughed out tissue to identify the exact area, revealing underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue, ensuring not to extend too far retroperitoneally to avoid the nearby ureters.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the mesentery, maintaining proximity to the appendix to avoid injuring the vessels supplying the small bowel.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the mesentery, staying close to the appendix to avoid injuring the vessels supplying the small bowel.

The surgeon continues to dissect edematous tissue, producing smoke in the surgical field. They carefully identify and coagulate the correct tissue planes, freeing the appendix and revealing the appendicular artery.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the edematous tissues, producing some smoke in the field. They carefully identify and separate the tissue planes, coagulating and sharply cutting to free the appendix. The appendicular artery becomes visible during this process.
The surgeon uses a bipolar electrocautery device to coagulate a visible vessel, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bipolar electrocautery device to coagulate a vessel near the appendix, ensuring hemostasis during the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon achieves good hemostasis using a bipolar instrument, effectively controlling minimal bleeding in the surgical field to maintain visibility and facilitate precise dissection.

The surgeon achieves good hemostasis using a bipolar instrument, effectively controlling minimal bleeding in the surgical field to maintain visibility and facilitate precise dissection of the appendix.
The surgeon demonstrates the complete coagulation of the vessel using a bipolar instrument, effectively controlling bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates that the vessel is completely coagulated, effectively controlling the bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses gauze to mop up the area, clearing the surgical field for better visibility.
The surgeon uses gauze to mop up the area around the appendix, clearing the field for better visualization.

The surgeon uses gauze to absorb fluids and perform dissection, providing traction and retraction of inflamed tissues without causing damage. The gauze helps maintain pneumoperitoneum, ensuring a clear surgical field.

The surgeon uses gauze instead of suction to absorb fluids and perform dissection, minimizing trauma to inflamed tissue. The gauze also aids in traction and tissue retraction, maintaining pneumoperitoneum for a clear surgical field.
The tissue retains its original color, aiding in the identification of the correct anatomical planes during the ulcer repair procedure.

The surgeon manipulates tissues using assisting hands, with an optical trocar placed through a ten-millimeter port at the umbilicus and another working port at the suprapubic level, providing adequate space and angle for manipulation and suturing.

The surgeon manipulates the tissue using assisting hands, with an optical trocar placed through a ten-millimeter port at the umbilicus and another working port at the suprapubic level. This setup provides adequate working space and angle for manipulation and suturing.
The surgeon removes tissues from the right lumbar region, positioned close to the umbilical line and away from the appendix.
The surgeon removes tissues in the right lumbar region, positioned close to the umbilical line and away from the appendix.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the appendix to release it, taking care to avoid thermal injury to the cecal wall, which could cause complications in the postoperative period.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the appendix to release it, taking care to avoid thermal injury to the cecal wall as they approach the base.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to coagulate tissues as fatty tissues are gradually removed. Fibrotic adhesions are further addressed during the procedure.
The surgeon removes the fatty tissues surrounding the appendix, using a bipolar device to coagulate any bleeding areas. Fibrotic adhesions are further dissected to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon lyses the remaining adhesions around the appendix, freeing it for appendectomy. The appendix is mostly freed with minimal adherent tissue, and the dissection proceeds in a cylindrical manner around the structure.

The surgeon lyses the phlegmon mass to free the appendix, revealing minimal adherent tissue. The dissection proceeds in a cylindrical manner around the appendix to ensure complete mobilization for the appendectomy.
The surgeon identifies a small fatty strand present between the appendix and surrounding tissues, highlighting the anatomical separation in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small fatty strand present between the appendix and surrounding tissues, indicating the progress of dissection.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection at an appropriate level, dividing the tissue with precise cuts.
The surgeon performs a sharp dissection at an appropriate level to divide the tissue surrounding the appendix.

The surgeon lyses small fatty strands surrounding the appendix, leaving only the appendicular attachment visible.
The surgeon lyses small strands of tissue surrounding the appendix, leaving only the appendicular attachment visible.
The surgeon has successfully dissected and taken down the fatty strands between the appendix and surrounding structures, revealing a clearer view of the appendix base and its junction with the cecum.
The surgeon has successfully dissected and taken down the appendix, with visible separation from surrounding fatty strands.
The surgeon applies traction to the appendix, revealing the internal tissues and providing a clearer view of the gangrenous and perforated areas at the junction with the cecum.
The surgeon applies traction to the appendix, revealing the surrounding tissues at the junction with the cecum, including visible gangrenous and perforated areas.
The surgeon incises the sloughed off tissue at the cecum level, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon incises the sloughed off tissue at the cecum level, revealing the gangrenous and perforated areas of the appendix base.
The surgeon carefully avoids the cecal wall while dividing the appendix, assessing the visible amount of sloughed tissue, infection, and unhealthy tissue present in the cecum.
The surgeon carefully avoids the cecal wall while dividing the appendix, assessing the visible slough, infection, and unhealthy tissue present at the cecum.
The surgeon exposes the cecum, revealing sloughed and unhealthy tissue, characterized by its discolored and necrotic appearance.
The surgeon exposes the cecum, revealing sloughed and unhealthy tissue, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts traction to improve exposure and visibility of the tissues, using ultrasonic shears to completely divide the appendix.
The surgeon adjusts traction to improve exposure and visibility of the tissues, using ultrasonic shears to completely divide the appendix.

After excising the appendix, the surgeon uses a suction device and a dramatic instrument to assess the level of sloughing in the cecum. The sloughing appears to extend deeper into the cecum in a tunneled fashion, while the surrounding cecal wall remains visibly healthy.

The surgeon carefully removes sloughed tissue to assess the vascularity and healthiness of the underlying tissue, preparing it for closure.
The surgeon carefully removes sloughed tissue to assess the vascularity and healthiness of the remaining tissue, preparing it for closure.
The surgeon examines the area inside the surgical field, identifying the extent of sloughed and unhealthy tissue. The tissue level is approximately determined for further revision.

The surgeon examines the surgical area, identifying the extent of sloughed and unhealthy tissue within the cecum. The team assesses the level of tissue damage to determine the appropriate point for revision.

The surgeon uses a suction instrument to remove pus from the stump area, revealing an opening into the cecum. The instrument enters the cecum freely, and pressing on the area causes additional pus to be expelled, indicating accumulation due to sloughing and unhealthy tissue.

The surgeon uses a suction instrument to remove pus from the stump area, revealing an opening into the cecum. The instrument enters the cecum freely, and additional pus is extracted, indicating previous accumulation due to sloughing and unhealthy tissue.
The surgeon examines the cecum to determine the extent of tissue damage, focusing on the junction between the appendix and the cecum to assess whether the damage extends further into the cecum.

After removing the pus, the surgeon uses suction to clear the area and applies traction on the tissue to identify the exact level of damage within the cecum.

The surgeon refashions the edges of the cecum, removing all gangrenous and sloughed out tissue to leave only healthy tissue visible.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to dissect and refashion the edges of the ulcer, ensuring hemostasis while removing any remaining unhealthy tissue.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to dissect and refashion the tissue edges, ensuring hemostasis while shaping the tissue to remove any remaining unhealthy areas.

The surgeon palpates the tip of the suction apparatus within the cecum, confirming the small size of the opening. The surrounding tissue appears healthy with good vascularity, and the previously unhealthy tissue has been removed. The suction device is used to extract collected fluid and pus from the area.

The surgeon guides the suturing instrument into the opening of the cecum, taking a thick bite of the inflamed, friable tissue to perform an interrupted suture, ensuring the tissue does not cut through.

The surgeon introduces a 29 mm half-circle needle through a 10 mm port, including the second posterior layer in a thick manner. The needle enters through the lumen to take bites for suturing.
The surgeon introduces a 29 mm half-circle needle through a 10 mm port. The needle is used to take thick bites, including the second posterior layer, ensuring entry through the lumen opening.
The surgeon precisely places the suture at the intended location on the cecum and ties it using a conventional laparoscopic knotting technique.
The surgeon precisely places the suture at the intended location and proceeds to tie a knot using conventional laparoscopic knotting techniques.
The surgeon performs a surgeon's throw followed by single throws to tighten the sutures, carefully approximating the tissues without applying excessive pressure to prevent tissue cut-through.
The surgeon performs a surgeon's throw followed by single throws to tighten and approximate the tissues, ensuring adequate tension without excessive pressure to prevent tissue cut-through.
The surgeon approximates the tissues using sutures to facilitate healing, ensuring the edges are aligned without excessive tension.
The surgeon approximates the tissue edges using sutures to facilitate healing, ensuring the tissues are aligned without excessive tension.
The surgeon has successfully approximated the tissues, resulting in a visible dumbbell formation as the sutures bring the edges close together.
The surgeon has successfully approximated the tissues, which are now visibly close together, with a clear dumbbell formation evident in the sutured area.
The surgeon takes closely spaced sutures to achieve a watertight closure, preventing the contents of the small bowel and cecum from spilling into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon takes closely spaced bites to achieve a watertight closure, preventing the contents of the small bowel and cecum from spilling into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon adjusts the limbs of the sutures, keeping one limb slightly longer to facilitate manipulation during challenging bite placements.

The surgeon attempts to introduce an instrument into a small opening, but the size of the opening prevents successful insertion.
The surgeon encounters difficulty inserting an instrument into a small opening, which is visibly too narrow for the instrument to pass through.

The surgeon takes a full-thickness bite with the suture needle, carefully controlling the needle's tip to achieve a precise exit point. The curvature of the needle is utilized during removal to minimize tissue trauma.

The surgeon tightens the knot, visibly approximating the tissues to achieve closure in the ulcer repair. The knotting technique is repeated to ensure a secure closure.

The surgeon ties the final knot for the second suture bite, ensuring secure tissue approximation. The third suture bite is taken, with precise control over the needle's exit point to achieve the desired level of tissue closure.
The surgeon completes the knotting for the second bite and proceeds with the third bite, carefully controlling the needle's exit point to ensure it aligns with the desired level.
The surgeon takes the third bite with the needle, reaching the end of the small opening in the tissue, ensuring precise control over the needle's exit point.
The surgeon completes the third suture bite, reaching the end of the small opening in the tissue.

The surgeon removes the needle and performs knotting using a double throw surgeon's knot followed by single throws, ensuring the knot is secure and tight enough to approximate the tissues without cutting through them, promoting effective wound healing.

The surgeon removes the needle and performs knot tying using a double throw surgeon's knot followed by single throws, ensuring the knot is secure and the tissues are well approximated to promote wound healing.
The surgeon uses a multifilament suture, such as Vicryl, to continue the repair process, ensuring secure tissue approximation.
The surgeon ties the final knot of the suture, securing the closure of the repaired ulcer site.
The surgeon ties the final knot of the stitch, ensuring secure tissue approximation for effective wound closure.
The surgical field is kept clean as the surgeon identifies the omentum and pulls it close to the cecum. A suture bite is taken on the cecal wall, precisely at the edge of the existing suture line.
The surgical field is kept clean as the surgeon identifies the omentum and pulls it close to the cecum. A suture is placed on the cecal wall, just on the edge of the existing suture line.
The surgeon takes a seromuscular bite, ensuring not to penetrate the lumen, followed by a bite in the omentum to perform a pexy, similar to the technique used for a duodenal perforation.

The surgeon takes a seromuscular bite in the appendiceal tissue, ensuring not to penetrate the lumen, followed by a bite in the omentum to perform a pexy, similar to the technique used for duodenal perforation.

The surgeon tightens the sutures along the suture line, aiming to secure the area and minimize spillage and peritonitis. The omentum is positioned to adhere to the suture line, providing additional support to localize any potential leakage.
The surgeon tightens the sutures along the suture line, ensuring the omentum adheres closely to control spillage and localize any potential peritonitis.
The surgeon secures the omentum in place over the suture line, ensuring it remains fixed rather than loosely draped.
The surgeon secures the omentum in place by tying knots to keep it positioned over the suture line, ensuring it remains stable rather than simply draping it over the area.
The final surgical view shows additional omentum being placed into the RAF fossa to help control the inflammatory process.
The surgeon places additional omentum into the right iliac fossa, aiming to control the inflammatory process visually evident in the surgical field.

The surgeon introduces an endobag into the abdominal cavity, displaying the two open limbs of the bag.

The surgeon places the dissected portion of the appendix into an endo bag and retrieves it, removing the appendix in a piecemeal manner to prevent spillage and minimize complications.

The surgeon places the dissected portion of the appendix into an endobag, carefully retrieving it to prevent spillage. The appendix is removed in a piecemeal manner within the bag to minimize complications and ensure proper wound healing.
The surgeon places additional omentum into the right iliac fossa (RAF) and inserts a drain in both the RAF and pelvis. The procedure concludes with the closure of the surgical ports using sutures.
The surgeon places a drain in the right iliac fossa (RAF) and the pelvis, then closes the surgical ports with sutures, marking the end of the appendectomy procedure.
